name: infrastructure
description: Infrastructure as Code patterns for deploying Guts nodes using Terraform, Docker, and Kubernetes
capabilities: Create Terraform modules for cloud infrastructure provisioning
Write multi-stage Docker builds for optimized container images
Configure Docker Compose for local multi-node development
Define Kubernetes StatefulSets for production node deployment
Implement monitoring stacks with Prometheus, Loki, and Jaeger
Apply security best practices for TLS, secrets, and RBAC
author: AbdelStark
securitySummary: The static findings are false positives. This is a documentation-only skill containing Infrastructure as Code examples. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are misidentified Terraform variable names (e.g., 'algorithm' as a variable name, not actual crypto usage). 'Ruby/shell backtick' flags are markdown code block delimiters. The single true positive ('rm -rf') is a standard Docker cleanup command in a multi-stage build, not malicious.
valueStatement: Deploying decentralized infrastructure requires careful planning across multiple environments. This skill provides ready-to-use Terraform, Docker, and Kubernetes patterns for deploying Guts nodes consistently from local development to production.
userTitle: Deploy Guts nodes with IaC patterns
limitations: Does not execute infrastructure changes directly
Requires existing cloud credentials for Terraform deployments
Does not manage state files or remote state backends
Assumes basic familiarity with Terraform, Docker, and Kubernetes concepts

useCases.title: Set up multi-node devnet
useCases.description: Configure Docker Compose for local development with multiple Guts validator nodes

useCases.title: Deploy to Kubernetes
useCases.description: Create production-ready Kubernetes manifests with StatefulSets and persistent storage

useCases.title: Provision cloud resources
useCases.description: Design Terraform modules for AWS or other cloud providers

promptTemplates.title: Local devnet setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me a Docker Compose configuration for running a local Guts development network with 3 nodes

promptTemplates.title: Production deployment
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Kubernetes StatefulSet configuration for deploying Guts nodes in production with persistent storage

promptTemplates.title: Terraform module
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a Terraform module structure for deploying Guts nodes on AWS with proper networking

promptTemplates.title: Security hardening
promptTemplates.prompt: What security configurations should I apply to my Guts node deployment including TLS, secrets, and network policies

bestPractices: Use multi-stage Docker builds to minimize container image size and attack surface
Separate Terraform environments (dev, staging, prod) with isolated state backends
Apply network policies and pod security standards in Kubernetes for isolation
antiPatterns: Avoid hardcoding credentials in Docker or Kubernetes configurations
Do not expose node API ports directly to the public internet without TLS termination
Avoid running containers as root user in production deployments

faq.question: What cloud providers does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on cloud-agnostic patterns. Terraform modules reference AWS as an example but can be adapted to any provider.

faq.question: What are the minimum resources needed to run a Guts node?
faq.answer: The skill suggests t3.medium as a default instance type but actual requirements depend on your workload and consensus settings.

faq.question: How do I connect nodes in a multi-node setup?
faq.answer: Configure the GUTS_PEERS environment variable with the hostnames and P2P ports of all peer nodes.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using the example configurations?
faq.answer: The skill provides starting templates. Always apply security hardening including TLS, secrets management, and network policies before production use.

faq.question: Why are my containers failing to start?
faq.answer: Check that ports are not already in use, volumes have proper permissions, and environment variables are correctly configured for your network topology.

faq.question: How does this compare to manual deployment?
faq.answer: IaC provides consistency, repeatability, and version control for infrastructure. Manual deployments are error-prone and difficult to reproduce across environments.
